To: editors@sciam.com

From: Drew Endy <dendy@molsci.org>

Subject: regarding "Cybernetic Cells" p.53 August 2001

Cc: yin@engr.wisc.edu, ha@molsci.org

Bcc:

X-Attachments:

 

Letters to the Editor

Scientific American

415 Madison Avenue

New York, NY 10017-111

 

Dear Sir or Madam:

 

I just read a friend's copy of the newly-released August 2001 issue of Scientific American wherein I am quoted and my work referenced [at the beginning of the "Cybernetic Cells" article by W. Wayt Gibbs (p53-57)].

 

First, I would like to thank you and Mr. Gibbs for taking an interest in my work, and for providing timely and generally-accessible coverage of the recently re-invigorated field of biological modeling.

 

Second, I would like to address several errors in the article: 

 

(1) The implication that the T7 model didn't "suggest a hypothesis that forces the model builder to do an experiment" is false (p.54, right column).  Instead, the T7 model has been a leading example of using a model to construct strong experimentally testable hypotheses.  For example, see "Computation, prediction, and experimental tests of fitness for bacteriophage T7 mutants with permuted genomes" by Endy D, You L, Yin J, Molineux IJ in PNAS-USA, May, 9 2000; 97(10):5375-5380.  See the problem?  As written, the article implies that I believe the T7 model to be "useless" (by my own definition of the word even).  <emphasis>At this instant,  the T7 model continues to be actively refined and applied by John Yin and his colleagues at Madison where it helps them to suggest and explore experimentally testable hypotheses; to imply otherwise is damaging.</emphasis>

 

(2) The Molecular Sciences Institute is not associated with the University of California at Berkeley.  Instead, it is an entirely independent not-for-profit basic research entity (p.54, left column) that was specifically founded in order to avoid the problems which limit true multi-disciplinary work within a University setting. 

 

(3) Bacteriophage T7 has been studied at least since 1944, and continuously since about 1969.  2001-1969 = 32 years; 2001 - 1944 = 57 years.  I'm not sure what the quote "15 years of laborious experiments" refers to.

 

I regret that Mr. Gibbs and I were unable to confirm the specific details of his article prior to publication.  I was travelling extensively when Mr. Gibbs first contacted me in late April 2001 and, although I called his office shortly thereafter, upon completing my travels, we did not communicate subsequent to our initial conversation.

 

I hope that you would be willing to distill points (1) and (2) above into an appropriately short "letter to the editor" to be published in a future issue of the magazine. 

 

Thank you for your time and attention to this matter; thanks again for publishing a stimulating article (not to mention the very creative artwork).

 

Best regards,

 

Drew Endy, Ph.D.

The Molecular Sciences Institute

2168 Shattuck Avenue, Floor 2

Berkeley, CA 94704

 

cc (by email) John Yin and colleagues, Lauren Ha

[a copy of this letter is available on my website, http://www.molsci.org/~dendy/]

--
